reasoning:
Verdict: REJECT (10% confidence)

Module Scores:
  Sentinel (CWE/OWASP Web Application Security): 50/100 [Low risk] — 1 info
  Watchdog (OWASP LLM Top 10 / Cisco AI Threat Taxonomy): 99/100 [Info risk] — 1 info
  Guardian (NIST AI RMF / EU AI Act / UNICC Responsible AI): 45/100 [High risk] — 1 critical, 3 high, 1 medium, 1 low

Arbitration Triggers:
  • REJECT trigger — critical findings in Guardian: [guardian-1] Autonomous content-authenticity decisions with minimal or no human oversight (high-risk behavior)

Disagreement Resolution:
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Sentinel scored 50/100 (low risk) — Δ49 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Sentinel to maintain safety margins.
  ⚠ Risk-level conflict: Guardian rates high risk while Sentinel rates low risk on the same application. Council defers to Guardian's stricter assessment.
  ⚠ Watchdog scored 99/100 (info risk) while Guardian scored 45/100 (high risk) — Δ54 point divergence. Arbitration defers to the stricter assessment from Guardian to maintain safety margins.

Confidence Factors:
  • Base: 1/3 module(s) independently support REJECT — 30% baseline conviction
  • -30% confidence: 3 disagreement(s) between modules
  • Average module score across 3 completed module(s): 65/100 (reported for context — not directly used in confidence calibration)

--- LLM-Enhanced Council Narrative ---
All three assessors converge on a single systemic problem: the absence of repository and operational artifacts (see sentinel [sentinel-1-e08bdeae], watchdog [watchdog-1], and guardian-2), which blocks deterministic security, LLM, and governance reviews. There is clear agreement that undisclosed model endpoints, leaked keys, and missing model provenance are plausible supply-chain risks (watchdog [watchdog-1]; guardian-5) and that human-in-the-loop controls are required for automated content-moderation decisions (guardian-1, watchdog recommendation). These are immediate, high-priority remediation areas: provide the codebase, run secrets/dependency scans, and document governance and human oversight workflows.

There is a notable conflict in severity labels: Sentinel's assessment (50/100, 'low' risk) reflects an inability to complete code-level checks and thus a cautious outcome, while Guardian classifies the system as high regulatory risk given autonomous moderation and absent privacy/governance artifacts (guardian-1, guardian-4). This divergence underscores that missing artifacts produce false confidence in technical scoring while governance/regulatory analysis properly treats the described functionality (automated moderation, user-uploaded content) as high-impact. Finally, gaps remain that no module fully addressed: CI/CD and container/image supply-chain security, and concrete runtime monitoring/telemetry for model safety and rollback. Remediation should therefore prioritize: (1) immediate provision of source, manifests, and deployment config; (2) secrets/dependency and image/pipeline scans; and (3) explicit governance artifacts (model cards, DPIA, human-review flows) and production monitoring requirements to close both technical and regulatory exposures.

top_findings: No source code or artifacts provided for VeriMedia AI (Flask/Python, ML components)
sentinel.summary: VeriMedia AI (described as a Flask/Python app using ResNet/BERT, PostgreSQL, and deployed on AWS) could not be assessed because no source code, dependency manifests, or configuration artifacts were provided. Without these artifacts, Sentinel cannot validate high-impact areas such as authentication, file upload handling, secret leakage, dependency CVEs, or API protections.
sentinel.recommendation: 1) Immediately provide the codebase and artifacts listed in the remediation so a full static analysis can be run (ties to finding: No source code). 2) Until code is available, run and share local scan outputs: bandit, semgrep (Flask/OWASP rules), truffleHog/git-secrets, and pip-audit or Safety to identify secrets and dependency issues (ties to finding: No source code). 3) Stand up a staging instance with TLS and run OWASP ZAP scans and authenticated API tests, then provide the scan reports for a

top_findings: No LLM usage detected in provided artifacts — repository/source not provided (Supply Chain / Unknown LLMs)
watchdog.summary: VeriMedia AI — no LLM/LLM-integration source code was provided. The application claims ML/AI features (deepfake detection, NLP fact verification, automated moderation) but without repository artifacts I could not locate any explicit LLM technologies (e.g., OpenAI GPT-4o, Anthropic Claude, LangChain, HuggingFace transformers). This prevents a deterministic assessment of OWASP LLM risks and leaves the supply chain / undisclosed model usage as the primary concern.
watchdog.recommendation:
Top 3 priority remediation actions:

1) Provide full source and environment artifacts: upload repository or grant read-only access (include Flask app files, workers, Dockerfile, requirements.txt, CI/CD configs). This enables line-level analysis for prompt injection (OWASP-LLM01), insecure output handling (OWASP-LLM02), and other LLM risks.

2) Run an immediate secrets and dependency scan: use git-secrets/truffleHog/detect-secrets and pipdeptree or safety to identify pinned/unpinned LLM libraries

top_findings: Autonomous content-authenticity decisions with minimal or no human oversight (high-risk behavior)
No governance or developer documentation (README, architecture, model card placeholders missing)
No bias testing or fairness evaluation for demographic or population-level impacts
guardian.summary: VeriMedia AI (Flask-based media verification platform using ResNet and BERT, PostgreSQL, and AWS) currently lacks foundational governance controls: there are no repository governance files (README, privacy policy, model cards), no bias/fairness testing, no documented human oversight, and no data protection artifacts. These gaps place the system in a high regulatory risk posture under the EU AI Act and deficient across NIST AI RMF GOVERN/MAP/MEASURE/MANAGE functions and UNICC Responsible AI princ
guardian.recommendation: Top 3 priority actions: 1) Establish immediate human oversight controls (finding: human_oversight) — add docs/human-oversight.md, implement src/human_review.py and an emergency kill switch; map to EUAI-OVERSIGHT and NIST-GOV-1. 2) Remediate privacy and data governance (finding: privacy) — create docs/privacy-policy.md, policies/DPIA.md, config/data-retention.yml, and consent logging in src/consent_manager.py to meet EUAI-DATA-GOVERNANCE and UNICC-PRIVACY. 3) Implement bias and model governance (
